title: Session 2 — Vision, Research & Team
subtitle: Session 2 · FREEDOM: Focus vision, Run research, Enlist team—toward Enhance your brand
theme: FREEDOM: Focus vision, Run research, Enlist team—toward Enhance your brand
objective: Ground participants in mission, vision, WHY, budget, and timeline; run humble research with real names; then enlist prayer and partnership teams (not solo fundraising), clarify give/get and math, identify people and invitations—so they leave with a starting prayer team, named roles, and a first action toward brand clarity next.

slides.title: Focus Your Vision
bullets: Mission, vision, and why tell your calling story
Budget and timeline make the ask concrete
slides.trainerCadence:
Welcome back—this block is the first step on the FREEDOM path: Focus your vision. We are building the internal clarity that shapes how you speak, pray, and invite partners.

By the end you should say—in plain language—what you are called to do, where you are headed, why it matters, what monthly support needs to look like, and what season you are aiming for.

Everything on screen is conceptual; the sentences that live in your mouth get written in the workbook. Messy drafts are welcome—polish comes later.
slides.trainerTransition: Let’s name what focus your vision means and why clarity is not optional.
slides.trainerScriptNotes:
- Connect to Session 1 without re-teaching it; FREEDOM “F” can be a quick nod.
- Watch anxiety when you say budget or timeline; normalize planning as faithfulness.
- Reinforce workbook-only drafting; no slide mirroring in participant mode.
slides.transitionCue: Teach introduction and why clarity matter before drafting mission and vision.

slides.title: Why Focus Your Vision?
bullets: Clarity now saves confusion later
Strong partnership follows clear vision
slides.trainerCadence:
Focus your vision means you can answer—without panic—what you are inviting someone into.

Clarity is not about being clever; it is about being faithful with language. When you are vague, you sometimes protect yourself from evaluation—which feels safe short term but gets expensive.

We are going to build five pieces: mission, vision, why, budget, timeline. Each one does a different job in the same story.
slides.trainerTransition: Start with mission: the framework, then a simple sentence pattern, then vision on its own beat.
slides.trainerScriptNotes:
- Distinguish vision work from graphic design.
- Name the cost of vague language without shaming.
- Invite resonance: where does it feel muddy?
slides.transitionCue: Move into Mission Statement—framework, then draft pattern, then vision.

slides.title: Mission Statement
bullets: What you do
Who you serve
How you serve
Where you serve
slides.trainerCadence:
Mission is your doing sentence—not your whole theology. These four anchors keep it concrete: what you do, who you serve, how, and where.

Land the framework before anyone tries to sound impressive.
slides.trainerTransition: Now give them a simple sentence skeleton they can say out loud.
slides.trainerScriptNotes:
- Keep it definitional; no vision language on this slide.
- Fast examples optional—clarity over cleverness.
slides.transitionCue: Advance to the mission draft pattern.

slides.title: Mission Statement
slides.trainerCadence:
Same title, different job: now they apply the framework in one line.

Walk the three blanks slowly—rough drafts are welcome.
slides.trainerTransition: Mission names the doing; vision names the horizon—next slide.
slides.trainerScriptNotes:
- Do not mix vision into this slide.
- Optional: one live example, then let them think.
slides.transitionCue: Advance to vision—separate beat from mission.

slides.title: Vision Statement
bullets: Future you long to see God bless
Orients plans and partnerships
Not the same as a funding goal
slides.trainerCadence:
Vision is the horizon—not a dollar line. It should orient your calendar and partnerships without pretending you control outcomes.

Keep mission and vision in separate sentences so partners hear two clear ideas.
slides.trainerTransition: Under that picture sits personal conviction, then numbers, then season—next we stack why, budget, and timeline.
slides.trainerScriptNotes:
- Separate vision from numeric goals—budget and timeline carry numbers.
- Normalize vulnerability; invite 3–5 year thinking.
slides.transitionCue: Bring conviction, monthly need, and timeline together on the next slide.

slides.title: Why, Budget & Timeline
bullets: Why: trust, not just logic
Budget: a real monthly number you can defend
Timeline: this season and the next ninety days
slides.trainerCadence:
Partners lean on mission and vision, but many ultimately lean in because they trust you—so we go deeper than slogans on WHY.

Partnership development needs a monthly number that is not imaginary: housing, insurance, ministry, margin, taxes where applicable—honor fear without letting it steer alone.

Timeline gives air: what season are you aiming for, and what belongs in the next ninety days? You should leave this block with working drafts for mission, vision, why, monthly direction, and horizon.
slides.trainerTransition: Before research, take a few minutes in small groups to process what you drafted—from Mission Builder through Timeline Reflection.
slides.trainerScriptNotes:
- Protect against oversharing in the room; workbook is private for WHY.
- Normalize ranges; discourage comparison.
- Next slide: mini breakout—then Run Your Research opens.
slides.transitionCue: Open short breakouts—then advance to Run Your Research.

slides.title: Discuss: Focus Your Vision
bullets: Groups of two to four; about five minutes
Use the prompts on screen
Say your draft out loud—honesty over polish
slides.trainerCadence:
Open your rooms—two to four people, about five minutes. Connect what you actually wrote—not a speech.

Work through the prompts: hardest line, what God may be surfacing, one honest phrase about your calling.

When you come back, we pick up Run your research—names, rhythms, dependence.
slides.trainerTransition: Close rooms with a thirty-second warning—then continue the FREEDOM path with research.
slides.trainerScriptNotes:
- Total window ~6 min including move; trim prompts if behind.
- Tie to workbook section names.
- Affirm courage over polish.
prompts: Which part was hardest to write—mission, vision, WHY, budget, or timeline—and what does that reveal?
Where do you sense God inviting deeper honesty or courage?
What is one phrase you would be willing to share that captures your calling right now?
slides.transitionCue: Close breakouts—advance to Run Your Research.
slides.interaction: In groups of 2–4, use the prompts on screen. Connect your answers to what you wrote from Mission Builder through Timeline Reflection.

instructions: Which workbook section was hardest—mission, vision, WHY, budget, or timeline—and why?
Where is God inviting deeper honesty?
What is one phrase that captures your calling today?
debrief: What theme surfaced in your group?
What encouragement did you hear?
What will you take into research?

slides.title: Run Your Research
bullets: Name storming
Following 3–5 missionaries
Interview them
slides.trainerCadence:

Say little here; let the slide land. Next we open mapping, discovery, and scheduling in detail.
slides.trainerTransition: Advance when the three moves feel named—then go to Map, Discover & Schedule.
slides.trainerScriptNotes:
- Keep the room on the three bullets; resist teaching the next slide early.
- Normalize awkwardness; curiosity over creepiness.
- Set expectation for real names in the workbook.
slides.transitionCue: Teach mapping, discovery, and scheduling as one movement.

slides.title: Map, Discover & Schedule
bullets: List real names by warm categories
Find and schedule conversations ethically
Let a ninety-day rhythm replace panic
slides.trainerCadence:
Most people know more people than they admit—they have not named them as part of a partnership journey.

Finding is not stalking; it is stewarding access God already gave. Keep tools lightweight: notes you will actually open when you are tired.

Timeline thinking sequences prayer and outreach so you do not try to do everything in one frantic week.
slides.trainerTransition: You do not need to invent posture from scratch—learn from people a few steps ahead.
slides.trainerScriptNotes:
- Validate small lists; shame kills honesty.
- Name ethics clearly.
- Connect lightly to vision timeline without repeating the whole exercise.
slides.transitionCue: Move to learning, observing, and interviewing as one skill stack.

slides.title: Learn, Observe & Interview
bullets: Learn posture from people ahead of you
Watch patterns—transfer principles, not personality
Short interviews; gratitude on the way out
slides.trainerCadence:
Identify people doing this well and learn patterns—not hero worship.

Observation sharpens when you know what you are looking for: value, invitation, gratitude, silence, stewardship of time.

Interviews are not mining secrets; they are learning posture. Fifteen minutes with clarity beats an hour of ramble.
slides.trainerTransition: Now name how you keep what you learn—and what to do when progress feels flat.
slides.trainerScriptNotes:
- Discourage celebrity-chasing; local examples often teach more.
- Offer question stems sparingly.
- Guard against envy.
slides.transitionCue: Consolidate tools, plateau, and dependence before the research breakout.

slides.title: Toolbox, Plateau & Dependence
bullets: Keep tools simple—retrieval beats sophistication
Plateaus are normal; check fear dressed as wisdom
Prayer names who God highlights next
slides.trainerCadence:
Your toolbox can be simple: a folder, a note, a short list—retrieval matters more than sophistication.

When lists stall, do not shame yourself—ask what God is forming and whether fear is masquerading as wisdom.

Research pairs with prayer: who is God highlighting? What conversation are you avoiding? Fruitfulness is God’s kindness; faithfulness is yours.
slides.trainerTransition: Before Enlist, process research in small groups—My Network through Interview Plan.
slides.trainerScriptNotes:
- Compassion for performance trauma.
- Optional short prayer before breakout.
- Next slide: mini breakout—then Enlist opens.
slides.transitionCue: Open short breakouts—then advance to Enlist Your Team.

bullets: Small groups; about five minutes
Name one person you will pray for this week
One research step you will actually take
slides.trainerCadence:
Open rooms—two to four people, about five minutes. Anchor in your workbook: network, research timeline, teachers, observations, interview plan.

Share one person you are praying for, name fear, commit to one doable step.

When you return, we enlist—not alone, not as theater, but as team.
slides.trainerTransition: Close rooms—then open Enlist your team: prayer, financial partners, advocacy.
slides.trainerScriptNotes:

- Bridge to theology of team.

slides.title: Enlist Your Team
bullets: Prayer partners first—then monthly partners
Advocates and connectors multiply the mission
Clear language before you ask for money
slides.trainerCadence:
We enter FREEDOM “E”: Enlist your team. Support raising stops sounding like a solo performance and starts sounding like the body of Christ.

You are inviting people into roles—prayer, partnership, advocacy—not collecting an audience.

We will anchor theology, reframe language, build from prayer outward, name financial and special teams, clarify give-and-get and math, then identify and invite—before brand aligns your message.
slides.trainerTransition: Start with God’s design: you were not made to pretend self-sufficiency.
slides.trainerScriptNotes:
- Normalize fear of bothering people.
- Energy hopeful, not hype.
- Foreshadow brand only at the end.
slides.emphasis: You were never meant to carry the mission alone—team is theology, not technique.
slides.transitionCue: Teach theology and partnership language as one foundation.

slides.title: Theology & Partnership Language
bullets: Needing help is normal—not failure
Partnership language is discipleship language
People give to mission they respect and understand
slides.trainerCadence:
Leadership is real; isolation is risky. God aligns people with His mission—needing help is not failure.

If you feel guilty asking, let theology correct it before tactics amplify it.

Partners give to what they understand, respect, and believe in—language matters because discipleship matters.
slides.trainerTransition: Build the team outward: prayer first, then financial partners, then advocates and connectors.
slides.trainerScriptNotes:
- One biblical example max if time.
- Compassion for shame.
- Connect to Session 1 reframe briefly if helpful.
slides.transitionCue: Stack prayer, financial, and special teams in one teaching movement.

slides.title: Prayer, Financial & Special Teams
bullets: Prayer partners get specifics and gratitude
Financial partners need clarity and fruit stories
Special roles carry advocacy beyond the check
slides.trainerCadence:
Before monthly asks, treat prayer partners as partners: specifics, gratitude, sustainable rhythm.

Financial asks need clarity without pressure; thank people sincerely and report fruit.

Special roles reduce the lie that only checks count—name what you need aloud.
slides.trainerTransition: Healthy partnership also names what partners receive and what you receive—then math stops being pure panic.
slides.trainerScriptNotes:
- Align with org policies.
- Sensitive to economic diversity.
- Bound roles so people are not unpaid staff by accident.
slides.transitionCue: Teach mutual partnership and math as one arc.

slides.title: Give / Get & Math Shift
bullets: Healthy partnership is mutual—not one-way extraction
Monthly math turns panic into a plan
Let numbers inform prayer—not shame
slides.trainerCadence:
Partners are not ATMs; relationship within mission means partners see God at work and you stay reachable.

Once give-and-get is clear, monthly gaps and gift ranges help you breathe.

Discernment comes next: warm relationships, fit, prayer before pursuit—not desperation hunting.
slides.trainerTransition: Identification becomes real when you speak a clear invitation.
slides.trainerScriptNotes:
- Ethical mutuality—no guilt manipulation.
- Avoid shaming slow progress.
- Light reference to org gift assumptions if applicable.
slides.transitionCue: Move to identifying people and crafting invitation.

Rehearse short—clarity is kindness

Invitation sounds like mission first: story, need, next step—with dignity for every no.

Rehearse with humility; fear is normal; clarity is kindness.

sections.title: Mission Builder
sections.subtitle: A first-draft mission line you can refine later
sections.intro: Use strong verbs and real people or places. Rough language is fine—you are aiming for clarity, not poetry.

sections.title: Vision Clarity
sections.subtitle: Where this work is headed
sections.intro: Think 3–5 years. What would you love to see God do through this assignment if He blesses it?

reflectionPrompts.label: Describe the future you are laboring toward in a few concrete sentences.
reflectionPrompts.placeholder: What would be true in people's lives, communities, or movements?

reflectionPrompts.label: What would make that future recognizably your calling—not any generic good work?

sections.title: Why Exercise
sections.subtitle: Surface, depth, and stakes
sections.intro: Go layer by layer. Short phrases are fine at first; let the last prompt pull honesty forward.

reflectionPrompts.label: Why does this calling matter to you—in plain, first-level language?
reflectionPrompts.placeholder: What do you tell people in one or two sentences?

reflectionPrompts.label: Strip the titles, budget, and schedule: why would you still care about this work?
reflectionPrompts.placeholder: What remains when externals are quiet?

reflectionPrompts.label: Who is affected if you shrink back—not only you?

sections.title: Budget Reflection
sections.subtitle: Honest monthly direction
sections.intro: A rough, prayerful number beats a polished fiction. Adjust later as you learn more.

reflectionPrompts.label: What monthly support amount are you initially working toward? (range or single number)
When you picture that number, what do you feel honestly—and what might God want to say into that?

sections.title: Timeline Reflection
sections.subtitle: Stewardship of time
sections.intro: Targets help you sequence invitations; they are not a contract with God.

reflectionPrompts.label: What target season or date are you aiming for full funding—or your next clear milestone?
What has to be true in the next 90 days for that timeline to stay credible?

sections.title: My Network
sections.subtitle: Real names by category
sections.intro: List first names (or initials if you must). You're not ranking people—just seeing who God has already placed near you.

reflectionPrompts.label: Family & close friends
reflectionPrompts.placeholder: Names, separated by commas or short lines

reflectionPrompts.label: Church, small group, ministry org
Work, school, professional network
Neighborhood, hobbies, community
Other (alumni, past teams, etc.)

sections.title: Timeline Reflection
sections.subtitle: Research & outreach pace
sections.intro: Separate from your full-funding timeline: when will you do the work of research, follow-up, and learning conversations?

reflectionPrompts.label: What 90-day horizon are you using for research and intentional conversations?
reflectionPrompts.placeholder: e.g. Now–June: map, learn, first interviews

reflectionPrompts.label: Who will you contact first—and by when (approximate is fine)?

sections.title: Who Can I Learn From
sections.subtitle: 3–5 practitioners
sections.intro: People who do partnership development with integrity—whom you could respectfully learn from (even one conversation counts).

reflectionPrompts.label: List 3–5 names (and context: e.g. field, church, org).
What is one question you most want to ask them?

sections.title: Observation Capture
sections.subtitle: What to watch for
sections.intro: When you observe or listen to someone who invites well, jot what stands out—language, rhythm, follow-up, tone.

reflectionPrompts.label: What principles or patterns do you notice (not copying personality—transferable insight)?
Phrases or ideas you want to borrow (adapted to your voice):

sections.title: Interview Plan
sections.subtitle: Respectful, specific, short
sections.intro: Plan one learning conversation you will actually initiate. Permission, gratitude, and a time limit go a long way.

reflectionPrompts.label: Who will you ask—and how will you invite them (email, call, coffee)?
Write 2–3 focused questions you will ask.
When will you send the invite or schedule—realistic date or week?

sections.title: Toolbox Anchor
sections.subtitle: One line to remember where your research lives
sections.intro: Optional: lock in where you'll keep notes, contacts, and prompts so nothing evaporates when life gets loud.

fillInItems.prompt: I will keep my research notes in ________ (app, folder, or notebook).
I will review my network list every ________.
My three go-to prompts or reminders are: ________, ________, and ________.

sections.title: Reframe
sections.subtitle: Partnership, not fundraising
sections.intro: Anchor your mouth and your heart. Rough words are fine.

fillInItems.prompt: I am inviting people into ________, not into paying my bills.
When I ask someone to partner, I am really asking them to ________.

sections.title: Prayer Team
sections.subtitle: Build it first
sections.intro: Name real people. Small is faithful.

reflectionPrompts.label: Who will you ask to pray for you and the mission? (list 3–7 first names)
How will you ask them (call, coffee, note)? What will you invite them to pray for specifically?
When will you send the invite or have the conversation?

sections.title: Existing Supporters
sections.subtitle: Who is already with you?
sections.intro: Partnership often grows from faithfulness to people already in the boat.

reflectionPrompts.label: Who already gives financially (even small)? Name them and how often you thank or update them.
Who prays for you or encourages you regularly—even if they don't give monthly?

sections.title: Advocacy Team Identification
sections.subtitle: Advocates · connectors · planners
sections.intro: Not everyone partners the same way. Name one person per role if you can—leave blank if unknown for now.

reflectionPrompts.label: Advocate (would speak your mission to others): name + why them
Connector (could introduce you to new relationships): name + possible door
Planner or helper (events, communication rhythm, follow-up systems): name + gift they bring

sections.title: Next Step
sections.subtitle: One move this week
sections.intro: Specific beats noble. One conversation, one note, one ask.

reflectionPrompts.label: The one action I will take in the next 7 days (who / what / when):
